answers.field_label: How Long Have You Known This Trainer
answers.value: I have known Sarah McAllister for just over 7 years, since early 2019, when she moved her first mare, Daisy, into my boarding and training facility, Harrington Ridge Stables, in Loveland, Colorado. At that time she was transitioning from primarily riding lesson horses to owning and managing her own horses, and I have watched her progress very closely since then. Over these 7 years I have seen her work with multiple types of horses, including green ranch-bred quarter horses, a sensitive off-track Thoroughbred gelding, and most recently two BLM mustangs gentled through the TIP program. Our relationship began as trainer–client, but over time has grown into more of a professional peer relationship; we frequently consult each other on training plans and facility improvements. I see her at the barn several times per week and have had continuous, year-round contact with her throughout this entire period, so my knowledge of her horsemanship and character is based on long-term, consistent interaction rather than a brief snapshot.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.field_label: How Often Do You See This Trainer Ride Or Work Horses
answers.value:
I see Sarah ride or work with horses in person an average of 3–5 times per week, depending on the season. She keeps two horses boarded at my facility full-time and often hauls in a third project horse on weekends during the training season. During the summer and fall, I see her nearly every weekday evening after her job as a veterinary technician, usually arriving around 5:30 p.m. and staying until 8:00 or 8:30 p.m. She typically rides or does groundwork with at least one horse every visit, and often two.

Beyond direct observation at my barn, I also see her at local open shows and mustang events 1–2 times per month from April through October. At these events, I have watched her work horses in busy warm-up arenas, handle loading and unloading at the trailer, and manage horses in unfamiliar show environments. Additionally, she regularly shares training videos with me—short cell phone clips of specific sessions—when she wants feedback on something she is working through, so even on days I don’t see her in person, I often see current footage of her handling and riding.

Over the years I have observed her in a wide variety of situations: initial haltering sessions with unhandled mustangs in a round pen, long-lining and desensitization in the arena, trail riding in groups and alone, hauling to clinics, and handling minor medical issues (like abscesses or scrapes). This frequent, varied exposure gives me a clear and detailed picture of her normal habits, safety practices, and overall approach to horse training.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.field_label: Do You Feel That Their Facilities Are Suitable For Working With A Wild Horse
answers.value:
yes. I have personally inspected and helped design portions of Sarah’s current setup at her small acreage property on the north side of Fort Collins, and I am very comfortable stating that her facilities are suitable—and actually quite well thought out—for working with a wild or very green horse.

She keeps her mustang projects at home but maintains a training arrangement with my facility for arena access. At her home property, she has a 60' x 80' heavy-gauge steel pipe pen that can be configured as either a larger turnout or divided into a 50' round pen and a smaller holding pen. The panels are 6 feet tall with closely spaced rails and no sharp edges, specifically chosen with wild horses in mind. The footing is decomposed granite with a light sand mix, which drains well and does not get slick or deep. All gates are secure, swing inward, and have double-chain closures plus snap latches to prevent accidental opening.

Her handling area is fully enclosed, with an additional perimeter fence (4-rail pipe and no-climb wire) so that if a horse were to push through a panel, it would still be contained on the property. She has installed solid-sided visual barriers along one side of the pen using plywood on the outside of the panels, which helps reduce external stimuli and keeps the environment calmer for a wild horse during the first weeks of handling.

For feeding and watering, she uses corner feeders that can be accessed from outside the pen, so she does not have to enter a tight space with an unhandled horse until it is safer to do so. Her water troughs are rubber tanks sunk partly into the ground and secured so they cannot tip. There is adequate lighting for evening work, but it is not harsh—she uses diffused LED flood lights mounted high enough that horses are not startled by shadows.

In addition, Sarah trailers to my facility 2–3 times per week to use our larger 100' x 200' outdoor arena, 60' round pen, and obstacle course once the horse is halter-broke and leading safely. She is very conservative about when she makes that transition; she does not haul a horse off-property until it is reliably halter broke, can be led, yield its hindquarters, and load in a trailer calmly.

From a safety and suitability perspective, I would rate her facilities as more than adequate for wild horse gentling: secure fencing, good footing, thoughtful handling access, and a clear plan for gradually increasing the horse’s exposure as it progresses. I would not hesitate to place a newly gathered mustang in her setup.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.field_label: Do You Have Any Concerns About The Horses In The Care Of This Trainer
answers.value:
no. I do not have any concerns about the horses in Sarah’s care; in fact, she is one of the more conscientious and detail-oriented owners and trainers I work with.

Her horses consistently maintain healthy body condition scores (typically in the 5–6 range), with good topline and appropriate muscling for their workload. I see them regularly, both at my facility and when I have visited her home setup, and I have never observed signs of neglect, overwork, or unresolved injuries. She is quick to involve a veterinarian or farrier if something seems off. As an example, last fall her mustang gelding, “Ranger,” came up slightly off on his right front after a particularly enthusiastic play session in turnout. The lameness was very mild, but rather than “riding him through it,” she gave him several days off, cold-hosed the leg, and scheduled a lameness exam with our local vet when the issue didn’t resolve as quickly as she expected. It turned out to be a minor hoof bruise, but her instinct was to err on the side of caution.

She keeps up-to-date records on vaccinations, deworming, and dental work. Because she works as a veterinary technician at a mixed animal practice, she is also very knowledgeable about basic first aid and preventive care. I have personally watched her calmly and correctly manage a superficial wire cut on a boarder’s horse when the owner was out of town: she cleaned and flushed the wound, applied an appropriate bandage, and coordinated follow-up care with the vet, all while keeping detailed notes for the owner.

In terms of handling and training methods, I have never seen her use excessive force or lose her temper with a horse. She is firm when necessary—especially about ground manners and personal space—but she is always fair and consistent. If a horse is confused or overwhelmed, her default is to simplify the task, break it down into smaller pieces, and give the horse time to process, rather than escalating pressure to the point of fear or panic.

If I were to note any “concern,” it would only be that she sometimes underestimates her own skills and can be overly self-critical, which in my experience leads her to take extra time and care rather than rushing. From the horses’ perspective, this tendency actually works in their favor, as she is extremely cautious about not pushing them faster than they are mentally or physically ready to go.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.field_label: Would You Recommend This Trainer To People With Unbroken Or Difficult Horses
answers.value:
yes. I would absolutely recommend Sarah to people with unbroken, green, or behaviorally challenging horses, and I already have done so on several occasions.

What sets Sarah apart in this area is her ability to combine patience and empathy with clear boundaries and a structured training plan. I have seen her work successfully with three particularly challenging horses in the past few years:

1) A nervous, ear-shy off-track Thoroughbred gelding named “Finn.” When Finn arrived, he was anxious about being touched around his head and neck and would throw his head violently when approached with a halter. Sarah spent several weeks doing nothing but quiet, reward-based handling—starting with simply standing in his stall and breathing with him, then gradually working up to touching his shoulder, neck, and eventually his poll. She broke the process into very small steps and rewarded each try. Within about six weeks, he was standing quietly for haltering, bridling, and ear handling, and he is now one of the steadiest horses in the barn.

2) A previously mishandled quarter horse mare, “Ruby,” who had learned to rear when pressured forward under saddle. Rather than confronting the rearing directly in the saddle, Sarah went back to groundwork: yielding hindquarters, moving shoulders, and teaching Ruby to respond reliably to light cues on the ground before reintroducing forward motion under saddle. She also identified and addressed a poorly fitting saddle that was contributing to Ruby’s discomfort. Over several months, the mare’s rearing behavior diminished and then disappeared, and she is now being used as a 4-H mount for a confident teenage rider.

3) A BLM mustang gelding, “Ranger,” who was completely unhandled when she adopted him through the TIP program. I observed his first haltering and the first time he accepted a hand on his neck. Sarah worked at his pace, using a lot of approach-and-retreat, careful reading of his body language, and very short, positive sessions. She kept detailed notes on each session—what worked, what didn’t, and what she would change next time. Within 90 days, he was halter broke, leading, loading, picking up his feet, and beginning light saddle work.

In all of these cases, Sarah demonstrated a strong understanding of both safety and learning theory. She is very conscious of where her own skill limits are; when an issue is beyond her comfort zone (for example, a confirmed dangerous behavior under saddle), she is not afraid to seek guidance from more specialized trainers, and she always prioritizes safety for both the horse and the handler.

For owners with unbroken or difficult horses who are looking for a trainer who will take the time to build a solid foundation rather than rushing to a quick “fix,” I consider Sarah an excellent choice. I have no hesitation in recommending her for TIP Challenge horses or similar projects.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.field_label: Personal Comments Regarding Skill Work Ethic Values Etc
answers.value:
Sarah is one of those rare individuals in the horse world who combines technical skill, humility, and an exceptionally strong work ethic with a genuine love and respect for the animals in her care.

From a skill standpoint, she has a solid grasp of groundwork, basic and intermediate ridden work, and practical problem-solving. I have seen her calmly handle situations that would rattle more experienced professionals—for example, when a young horse spooked at a flapping tarp near the arena gate and spun toward her, she maintained her composure, redirected his feet, and turned the incident into a teaching moment instead of a fight. Her timing with pressure and release is very good, and she has developed a soft, following seat that helps green horses stay balanced and relaxed.

Her work ethic is outstanding. Sarah works full time as a veterinary technician, yet she still manages to be at the barn consistently, even in bad weather or after long days. She does not cut corners: she warms up and cools down her horses properly, cleans her tack regularly, and keeps her feeding and turnout schedules consistent. When she was preparing Ranger, her mustang, for a previous TIP showcase, she came out early in the mornings before work at least three days a week to do short groundwork sessions in addition to her evening rides, because she felt he needed that extra repetition to build confidence.

In terms of values, Sarah consistently puts the horse’s long-term well-being ahead of short-term goals or ribbons. She has passed on shows and clinics when she felt a horse was not mentally ready for the environment, even when she had already paid entry fees. She is honest about where a horse is in its training and does not exaggerate its abilities to potential adopters or buyers. I have overheard her explaining to a prospective adopter that while a horse might be safe for an intermediate rider in the arena, it would still need a confident, experienced handler on the trail for another season or two. That level of transparency is, in my opinion, one of the clearest indicators of her integrity.

On a personal level, she is easy to communicate with, open to feedback, and respectful of barn rules and other people’s horses. When she uses my facility, she always leaves the arena and round pen in good order—poles put away, gates closed, manure picked up—and she is the first to volunteer to help set up or tear down obstacles for clinics and schooling shows. She has also stepped in multiple times to help less experienced boarders with basic handling issues, always in a kind, non-judgmental way.

In summary, I believe Sarah has both the technical ability and the character traits necessary to be an excellent participant in the TIP Challenge program. She is safety-conscious, observant, and deeply committed to doing right by the horses she works with. I trust her with my own horses and with my facility, and I have no hesitation in recommending her for this opportunity.
